title: Towards a world where no one is surprised by a natural disaster
gist: Google is deploying a portfolio of AI-powered climate and disaster-resilience tools — flood forecasting covering 2 billion people, wildfire satellite detection, hurricane prediction, and heat-mapping — built over a decade of research and now entering scaled commercial and government deployment. These products, partnered across 90+ countries and with UN agencies, represent a maturing platform play in climate-tech infrastructure that competes with and complements national meteorological services.

insights.claim: Google's Flood Hub now provides river flood forecasts covering 2 billion people across more than 150 countries, with predictions available up to seven days in advance, scaled from a single pilot region in Patna, India in 2018.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Flood Hub's scale — 2 billion people across 150+ countries — signals a defensible data moat that is difficult for any government or competitor to replicate quickly, and anchors potential monetization through public-sector contracts.
insights.quote: Today, forecasts on Flood Hub cover 2 billion people across more than 150 countries, in areas at risk for significant flood events.

insights.claim: Google developed a new AI-based flash flood prediction system for urban areas that provides up to 24-hour advance notice of rapid-onset events, trained using a proprietary 20-year dataset of public reports called Groundsource.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Proprietary training datasets (Groundsource) built from 20 years of historical records create a durable competitive barrier against new entrants in climate AI.
insights.quote: with our new AI-based methodology, Groundsource, we built a high-quality floods dataset based on 20 years of public reports, which we used to train a flash floods model.

insights.claim: Google open-sourced its flash floods dataset and hydrology framework, enabling external researchers, businesses, and local experts to build derivative solutions on top of Google's flood modeling infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Open-sourcing the framework expands the ecosystem and entrenches Google's underlying platform, potentially attracting commercial and government users who extend its data flywheel.
insights.quote: We've open sourced both the flash floods dataset and our hydrology framework, so researchers, businesses and local experts can build new solutions.

insights.claim: GiveDirectly and the International Rescue Committee used Google's flood forecasts in Nigeria and Bangladesh to distribute emergency cash ahead of rising waters, demonstrating real-world anticipatory action powered by AI predictions.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Demonstrated humanitarian use cases with named NGO partners validate the operational reliability of Google's forecast products and provide reputational and regulatory tailwinds for further government adoption.
insights.quote: GiveDirectly and the International Rescue Committee have used our flood forecasts to power anticipatory action, distributing emergency cash ahead of rising waters.
insights.locator: Ongoing support for a shared global mission section

insights.claim: Google's WeatherNext 2 model generates highly detailed hourly global weather forecasts — including wind speed, direction, precipitation, and pressure — in minutes, and during the 2025 hurricane season predicted cyclone paths and intensities days in advance with high confidence.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Speed and accuracy of WeatherNext 2 positions Google to displace or supplement legacy numerical weather prediction systems used by national meteorological agencies, opening a large government and insurance-sector market.
insights.quote: It can generate highly detailed hourly forecasts for the whole globe in minutes, and is capable of forecasting crucial weather variables including wind speed and direction, precipitation and pressure.

insights.claim: The U.S. National Hurricane Center used Google's WeatherNext model during Hurricane Melissa and predicted the Jamaican landfall five days ahead, enabling the Jamaican Met Service to issue early public warnings.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Adoption by the U.S. National Hurricane Center is a high-credibility government validation that strengthens Google's pitch for further public-sector weather-modeling contracts globally.
insights.quote: when the U.S. National Hurricane Center used our WeatherNext model, it predicted the Jamaican landfall five days ahead.
insights.locator: Ongoing support for a shared global mission section

themes.theme: Wildfire Detection and FireSat Satellite Constellation

insights.claim: Google co-developed FireSat with the Earth Fire Alliance and Muon Space, funded by Google.org, the Moore Foundation, and the Bezos Earth Fund; the first protoflight satellite reached orbit in 2025, and a full constellation of 50+ satellites would detect wildfires as small as 5x5 meters anywhere on Earth with 20-minute refresh rates.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: A 50+ satellite constellation for near-real-time wildfire detection is a capital-intensive infrastructure bet with potential insurance, reinsurance, and government emergency-management revenue streams once fully deployed.
insights.quote: A full FireSat constellation of 50+ satellites would be able to detect wildfires just 5 x 5 meters anywhere on earth, with updates every 20 minutes.

insights.claim: Google's AI-powered wildfire boundary tracking via satellite imagery in Search and Maps has expanded to 34 countries, including seven new countries added in the current year.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Geographic expansion of wildfire tracking into 7 new countries signals accelerating regulatory and partnership momentum and broadens the addressable market for downstream emergency-services contracts.
insights.quote: we have expanded to provide coverage in 34 countries, including seven new countries this year.

themes.theme: Extreme Heat and Urban Climate Intelligence

insights.claim: Google is applying AI to satellite and aerial imagery to map the reflectivity of buildings across urban environments, enabling cities to identify where cool roofs could reduce surface temperatures — a capability published in a scientific paper as of June 2026.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Urban heat mapping via AI creates a commercializable data product for city governments and real-estate developers assessing climate-adaptation costs, a nascent but growing market.
insights.quote: we're applying AI to satellite and aerial imagery to map the reflectivity of buildings across urban environments, as we just published.

insights.claim: Google's extreme heat alerts on Search cover people in over 100 countries and include safety tips sourced from the Global Heat Health Information Network.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Embedding authoritative heat-health guidance into Google Search at 100-country scale demonstrates how Google can serve as a public-health infrastructure layer, reinforcing its position in government and NGO partnerships.
insights.quote: Extreme heat alerts on Search provide warnings for people in over 100 countries, including safety tips from the Global Heat Health Information Network.
insights.locator: Real-time alerts and authoritative information section

themes.theme: Google Earth AI and Planetary Intelligence Platform

insights.claim: Google launched the Google Earth AI collection, which integrates climate and geospatial models to enable holistic 'planetary intelligence' — capable of jointly reasoning about imagery, population data, and environmental factors to answer complex disaster-response queries.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Google Earth AI positions Google as the connective tissue for multi-modal climate analytics, creating a platform that businesses and government agencies can build on, generating recurring data and API revenue.
insights.quote: We've brought together our climate and geospatial models in the Google Earth AI collection of models and datasets. It enables planetary intelligence.

insights.claim: Google's crisis information products — including SOS alerts on Search and Maps and Public Alerts in partnership with over 90 countries — connected people with crisis information over 10 million times per day on average in the prior year.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Ten million daily crisis-information interactions demonstrates a massive distribution advantage that no standalone climate-tech startup can replicate, cementing Google's role as the default emergency-information layer.
insights.quote: last year alone, Google helped connect people with crisis information over 10 million times per day, on average.
insights.locator: Real-time alerts and authoritative information section

themes.theme: Android Earthquake Alerts and Air Quality Infrastructure

insights.claim: The Android Earthquake Alerts System detects earthquakes and sends alerts to Android users before shaking reaches them, providing time to reach safety — leveraging the global Android device fleet as a distributed sensor network.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Using the Android device ecosystem as a planetary seismic sensor network is an asset no competitor with a smaller device installed base can replicate, reinforcing Android's strategic value beyond mobile OS.
insights.quote: The Android Earthquake Alerts System detects earthquakes and alerts Android users before shaking reaches them, to give people time to get to a safe place.
insights.locator: Real-time alerts and authoritative information section

insights.claim: Google provides real-time air quality data on Google Maps in over 30 countries to help users reduce exposure to pollution, expanding its environmental health data footprint.
insights.speaker: Yossi Matias
insights.so_what: Air quality data embedded in Google Maps creates a data layer with potential monetization through health-sector APIs, municipal contracts, and insurance underwriting tools.
insights.quote: Up-to-date air quality data is available on Google Maps in over 30 countries.
insights.locator: Real-time alerts and authoritative information section
